title: Getting random 403 Forbidden errors on the dashboard
body: I keep getting 403 errors when I try to access the TaskFlow dashboard. It's not every time — sometimes it loads fine, sometimes I get a 403 page. It seems totally random. My permissions haven't changed and other people on my team say they're seeing it too. Started happening a couple days ago.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body:
Thanks for reporting this! To help narrow down what's going on, can you describe what's happening right before you see the 403?

A) You log in and the dashboard immediately shows 403
B) You're already using the dashboard and it suddenly starts returning 403 (e.g., after being idle or navigating)
C) It depends on which part of the dashboard you're accessing — some pages work, others don't
D) Something else

Also — when it does happen, does refreshing the page fix it, or does the 403 persist for a while?

comments.author: reporter
comments.body:
It's kind of a mix of A and B honestly. Sometimes I log in and immediately get a 403, other times I'm clicking around and then suddenly hit one. It doesn't seem tied to a specific page — it's more like any request can randomly fail.

And yeah, refreshing usually fixes it! Sometimes I have to refresh a couple times but eventually it loads. It's really annoying though because it keeps happening throughout the day. I was thinking maybe it's some kind of network issue on our end but my coworkers with the same role are seeing it too.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body:
Thanks for the detail — the pattern you're describing (random 403s across any page, clears on refresh, multiple people affected, started recently) is consistent with either a backend node that's misconfigured behind a load balancer, or a recent change to how sessions/permissions are validated.

Do you know if anything changed around the time this started — roughly two days ago? For example:

A) A new version of TaskFlow was deployed
B) Your team's roles or permissions were updated (even if they look the same now)
C) Something changed in your SSO/identity provider setup
D) Not sure — nothing obvious on our end

This will help us figure out whether we're looking at a regression vs. an infrastructure issue.

comments.author: reporter
comments.body: Now that you mention it, I think our roles were updated recently — I was assigned a new 'analyst' role around the same time this started. So probably B. I'm not sure if there was a deployment or anything like that, I wouldn't normally know about that stuff. But the timing does line up with when I got the new role. My coworkers who are seeing the same thing also have the analyst role, so maybe that's related? I just assumed it was a network thing since it's so intermittent.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body:
That's really helpful — the timing match with the analyst role is a strong signal. Here's what I think is happening: the new 'analyst' role is likely involved in the 403s, but the *intermittent* nature suggests it might be a conflict or caching issue rather than the role simply lacking permissions (if it just lacked permissions, you'd get 403 every time).

One more thing that would help us pin this down: was the analyst role **added alongside** your existing role, or did it **replace** a previous role?

A) It was added — I still have my previous role(s) too (e.g., 'member' + 'analyst')
B) It replaced my old role (e.g., I was 'member' before, now I'm just 'analyst')
C) Not sure — I'd have to check

This will tell us whether we're looking at a role conflict vs. a missing-permissions issue.

comments.author: reporter
comments.body: I think it replaced my old role — I used to be a 'viewer' or maybe 'editor', I'm not totally sure, but now I just have 'analyst'. So probably B. Though I'm not 100% certain if the old one was removed or if they both exist, I'd have to check with my manager who set it up.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body: [RESOLVED] {"title":"Intermittent 403 errors for users assigned the new 'analyst' role","problem":"Users who were recently assigned the 'analyst' role (approximately 2 days ago) are experiencing random 403 Forbidden errors across all dashboard pages. The errors are not page-specific, affect multiple users with the same role, and resolve temporarily on refresh. The pattern suggests a permission evaluation inconsistency rather than a blanket denial.","root_cause_hypothesis":"The 'analyst' role likely replaced users' previous roles (e.g., 'viewer' or 'editor'). The intermittent nature suggests either: (1) a permission/session cache that sometimes serves stale authorization decisions — some backend nodes or cache entries still reference the old role while others have the new one, or (2) the 'analyst' role's permissions were incompletely configured and a race condition in permission resolution causes sporadic failures. A load-balanced setup where not all nodes have consistent permission state would explain why refreshing sometimes works immediately and sometimes takes multiple attempts.","reproduction_steps":["Identify a user with only the 'analyst' role assigned","Log in and access the dashboard repeatedly","Observe that some requests return 403 while others succeed","Check whether the 403 correlates with hitting different backend nodes (if load-balanced) or with cache timing"],"environment":"Production TaskFlow instance, multiple users affected, all with 'analyst' role. No confirmed deployment changes, but role assignment change ~2 days ago aligns with symptom onset.","severity":"high","impact":"All users with the 'analyst' role are intermittently locked out of the dashboard throughout the day, disrupting their workflow. Multiple team members affected.","recommended_fix":"1. Check the permission/role configuration for 'analyst' — verify it grants dashboard access equivalent to the roles it replaced. 2. Inspect the permission caching layer (session cache, CDN, or reverse proxy) for stale entries tied to the old role. 3. If load-balanced, check whether all backend nodes have consistent role/permission definitions. 4. Review the role migration — confirm the old role was cleanly removed and the new role's permissions were fully propagated. 5. Consider flushing permission caches for affected users as an immediate mitigation.","proposed_test_case":"Assign a test user the 'analyst' role (removing any previous role), then issue 50+ sequential authenticated requests to the dashboard API and verify all return 200. Repeat against each backend node individually if load-balanced.","information_gaps":["Exact previous role name (reporter unsure if it was 'viewer' or 'editor')","Whether the old role was explicitly removed or still coexists in the system","Whether a deployment occurred around the same time as the role change","Specific backend architecture details (load balancer config, caching layer)"]}
